description:
<context-digest>
<principles>Design for Change · Design for Simplicity · Validate Your Work · Make Intent Explicit · Prefer Reversible Decisions</principles>
<concerns>hugo-hextra | demo-asciinema</concerns>
<practices>Theme version: Hextra v0.12.1 — pinned in website/go.mod · Hugo version: 0.159.2 extended — pinned in .github/workflows/pages.yml · Current demos: helix-quickstart (full lifecycle), helix-concerns (drift detection), helix-evolve (requirement threading) · Agent harness: ddx agent run --harness claude — not claude -p</practices>
<governing>tests/validate-skills.sh — A deterministic repo test executes bin/helix without manually pre-setting HELIX_ROOT, verifies the wrapper delegates to scripts/helix via plugin-root resolution, and runs from the normal FEAT-004 plugin-packaging validation surface together with bash tests/validate-skills.sh or bash tests/test-install.sh.</governing>
</context-digest>

Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: tests/validate-skills.sh:73
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: Commit 783ef571 adds new runtime logic in bin/helix to derive HELIX_ROOT from CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT or the wrapper path, but the deterministic verification surface still does not execute that wrapper. tests/validate-skills.sh only checks that bin/helix exists and is executable, and tests/test-install.sh exercises the copied HOME/.local/bin/helix launcher with HELIX_ROOT injected manually instead of the plugin wrapper path. That leaves the new delegation logic effectively code-inspection-only: a regression in bin/helix path resolution can slip through the documented plugin packaging lane while FEAT-004 and the implementation plan both require a bin/helix delegation test.
Suggested fix: Add a checked-in deterministic test that runs bin/helix through the plugin-style path, proves it sets HELIX_ROOT from CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT or wrapper resolution, and wire that check into the standard plugin packaging validation lane.
title: correctness: add deterministic coverage for bin/helix delegation

acceptance: A bead whose ADRs are discovered only through secondary matching produces a non-empty <adrs> section with summarized ADR content, and an automated test covers that path.
description:
<context-digest>
<principles>Design for Change · Design for Simplicity · Validate Your Work · Make Intent Explicit · Prefer Reversible Decisions</principles>
<concerns>hugo-hextra | demo-asciinema</concerns>
<practices>Define the site's target audience and what content they need · Theme version: Hextra v0.12.1 — pinned in website/go.mod · Identify which workflows need demo reels — prioritize the "first 5 minutes" experience · Current demos: helix-quickstart (full lifecycle), helix-concerns (drift detection), helix-evolve (requirement threading)</practices>
<governing>scripts/refresh_context_digests.py — def summarize_adr(root: Path, ref: str) -> str: path = resolve_artifact_path(root, ref) if path is None or not path.exists(): return "" markdown = read_text(path) adr_id = extract_doc_id(markdown) or strip_markdown(ref) title_match = re.search(r"^#\s+(.*)$", markdown, re.MULTILINE) title = strip_markdown(title_match.group(1)) if title_match else adr_id title = re.sub(rf"^{re.escape(adr_id)}:\s*", "", title) decision = choose_best_clause( iter_markdown_clauses(extract_section(markdown, "Decision")), "decision selected approach", ) rationale = extract_adr_rationale(markdown) summary = f"{adr_id} {title}" if decision: summary += f" — {decision}" if rationale: summary += f" Why: {rationale}" return summary</governing>
</context-digest>

Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: scripts/refresh_context_digests.py:340
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: The new secondary ADR discovery path is dead code. `secondary_adr_matches()` returns bare filenames like `ADR-001-supervisory-control-model.md`, but `build_adrs()` passes those strings to `summarize_adr()`, which calls `resolve_artifact_path()`. That resolver only handles direct repo-relative paths, extracted document IDs, or filename stems, so a bare filename with `.md` never resolves. In the live repo, `secondary_adr_matches()` returns both ADR filenames for a workflow bead, while `build_adrs()` still returns `[]`. That means the FEAT-006 fallback path for ADRs not wired into concerns silently never contributes an `<adrs>` section.
Suggested fix: Make secondary matches return repo-relative paths or ADR document IDs, or teach `resolve_artifact_path()` to resolve bare ADR filenames. Add a test that exercises the secondary-match path end to end so the fallback cannot regress silently.
title: correctness: fix secondary ADR discovery in digest helper

acceptance: Governing HELIX docs identify `ddx agent execute-loop` as the primary queue-drain substrate and `ddx agent execute-bead` as the bounded execution primitive; HELIX bead guidance requires deterministic success-measurement criteria suitable for DDx-managed close-with-evidence; any remaining implementation gap between current HELIX behavior and the shipped DDx execution surfaces is captured as concrete follow-on beads with deterministic acceptance.
description:
<context-digest>
<principles>Design for Change · Design for Simplicity · Validate Your Work · Make Intent Explicit · Prefer Reversible Decisions</principles>
<concerns>hugo-hextra | demo-asciinema</concerns>
<practices>HELIX should consume DDx substrate capabilities rather than shadow-implement them · Treat execute-bead as the canonical bounded execution primitive when DDx ships it · Prefer queue cleanup that closes already-satisfied findings instead of carrying stale review debt</practices>
<governing>CONTRACT-001 · FEAT-011 · TD-011 — HELIX already specifies ddx agent execute-bead as the bounded DDx-managed execution primitive, but live workflow docs and implementation surfaces still mix that contract with direct ddx agent run orchestration.</governing>
</context-digest>

DDX now ships `ddx agent execute-bead <bead-id> [--from <rev>] [--no-merge]` locally. Align HELIX governing docs and queue policy to that shipped capability, identify the remaining gap between current HELIX implementation and the contract already written in FEAT-011 / TD-011 / CONTRACT-001, and queue any concrete follow-on implementation work needed to start using execute-bead in the HELIX execution path.

Scope:
- Reconcile HELIX governing docs that still describe direct `ddx agent run` orchestration for bounded bead execution.
- Distinguish DDx-managed execute-bead flows from plain `ddx agent run` usage for planning, review, and non-managed prompts.
- Review the outstanding HELIX queue for stale findings already satisfied, findings superseded by execute-bead adoption, and follow-on work that becomes newly relevant once execute-bead is available.
- Create any follow-on build/test beads needed for actual helix-run / helix-build adoption after the contract is aligned.
title: Align HELIX execution contract to shipped DDx execute-loop / execute-bead capabilities

In either case, the deploy artifact metadata and template agree on the required inputs for monitoring-setup generation.
description:
<context-digest>
<principles>Design for Change · Design for Simplicity · Validate Your Work · Make Intent Explicit · Prefer Reversible Decisions</principles>
<concerns>hugo-hextra | demo-asciinema</concerns>
<practices>Theme version: Hextra v0.12.1 — pinned in website/go.mod · Hugo version: 0.159.2 extended — pinned in .github/workflows/pages.yml · Current demos: helix-quickstart (full lifecycle), helix-concerns (drift detection), helix-evolve (requirement threading) · Agent harness: ddx agent run --harness claude — not claude -p</practices>
<governing>workflows/phases/05-deploy/artifacts/monitoring-setup/meta.yml — The monitoring-setup artifact contract is order-consistent: either runbook is a valid earlier-or-equal prerequisite for monitoring-setup, or workflows/phases/05-deploy/artifacts/monitoring-setup/{meta.yml,template.md} no longer require runbook links before runbook is created. In either case, the deploy artifact metadata and template agree on the required inputs for monitoring-setup generation.</governing>
</context-digest>

Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: workflows/phases/05-deploy/artifacts/monitoring-setup/meta.yml:57 and workflows/phases/05-deploy/artifacts/monitoring-setup/template.md:62
Category: integration
Severity: medium
Description: Commit 839e82e removes `runbook` from `monitoring-setup.dependencies.requires`, but the artifact contract still assumes the runbook exists before this creation step. `monitoring-setup` remains `creation_order: 2` while `runbook` is `creation_order: 3`, yet the template still requires a `Runbook Links` section and the validation check still asks the document to reference the runbook. That leaves the deploy workflow internally inconsistent: the scheduler can now generate `monitoring-setup` before `runbook`, but the generated document is still asked to link to a later artifact.
Suggested fix: Make the metadata and template agree on whether `runbook` is an input. Either restore a backward-compatible prerequisite by reordering the artifacts, or keep the current ordering and relax the monitoring contract so incident response can be completed without pre-existing runbook links.
title: integration: align monitoring-setup runbook contract with deploy ordering

acceptance: HELIX design docs, artifact templates, and phase guidance include a first-class contract artifact with explicit selection rules and naming guidance; contract template support exists in the workflow bundle; guidance covers non-HTTP protocol/interface contracts such as telemetry schemas; ADR/SD/TD/contract boundaries are unambiguous enough that a telemetry export contract can be placed without artifact-taxonomy confusion
description:
<context-digest>
<principles>Design for Change · Design for Simplicity · Validate Your Work · Make Intent Explicit · Prefer Reversible Decisions</principles>
<concerns>hugo-hextra | demo-asciinema</concerns>
<practices>Theme version: Hextra v0.12.1 — pinned in website/go.mod · Hugo version: 0.159.2 extended — pinned in .github/workflows/pages.yml · Current demos: helix-quickstart (full lifecycle), helix-concerns (drift detection), helix-evolve (requirement threading) · Agent harness: ddx agent run --harness claude — not claude -p</practices>
<governing>workflows/phases/02-design/README.md — HELIX design docs, artifact templates, and phase guidance include a first-class contract artifact with explicit selection rules and naming guidance</governing>
</context-digest>

HELIX design guidance already treats contracts as a first-class artifact category (`docs/helix/02-design/contracts/`), and the design gate expects contract docs to exist, but the workflow bundle does not currently ship a dedicated contract artifact template/prompt/meta alongside solution-design, technical-design, ADR, spike, and PoC templates. At the same time, live HELIX repos are using both `API-*` and `CONTRACT-*` identifiers for documents in the contracts directory. This leaves contract-shaped specs underspecified exactly when we need them for reusable protocol/interface definitions such as OpenTelemetry telemetry capture standards, DDx/HELIX boundary contracts, execution-document conventions, CLI/library mutation surfaces, and other cross-project schemas that should be shareable without dragging along feature-level or solution-level baggage.

Request: add an explicit first-class contract artifact type to HELIX design-phase tooling and documentation. The artifact must be suitable for normative external interface specifications, not just HTTP APIs. It should support protocol/data/interface contracts such as telemetry schemas, cross-project ownership boundaries, authoring conventions, CLI/library mutation surfaces, and other reusable contracts. The artifact definition should make clear that ADRs carry the decision/rationale (WHY), solution designs carry the feature-level approach (HOW for a feature), technical designs carry one bounded slice, and contracts carry the authoritative field-by-field/schema-level/interface-level specification that another team could implement against directly.

Requirements from this discussion:
1. Define clear selection rules for when to use ADR vs Solution Design vs Technical Design vs Contract.
2. Ship a contract template/prompt/meta in the HELIX workflow bundle.
3. Clarify identifier and naming guidance: whether `API-*` remains a subtype, whether `CONTRACT-*` is the canonical prefix, or whether both are supported with explicit scope boundaries.
4. Make the contract artifact suitable for non-HTTP surfaces, including telemetry/export contracts.
5. Require contracts to carry exact keys/fields, types, units, enums, precedence rules, formulas, error semantics, examples, and validation checklists where relevant.
6. Ensure the design gate/enforcer language matches the chosen artifact naming and template support.
7. Preserve the ability for contracts to be shared across projects without inheriting unrelated feature or solution baggage.
8. Document how contracts should be referenced from ADRs and solution designs so the normative schema lives in one place rather than being duplicated across artifacts.
title: Add first-class contract artifact support in HELIX design phase

acceptance: python3 -m unittest discover -s tests -p 'test_refresh_context_digests.py' includes a case where the first override bullet duplicates the first library bullet, and select_digest_practices(['Library A', 'Library B'], ['Library A', 'Override C'], 2) still returns one library practice plus one override-specific practice; git diff --check passes.
description:
<context-digest>
<principles>Design for Change · Design for Simplicity · Validate Your Work · Make Intent Explicit · Prefer Reversible Decisions</principles>
<concerns>hugo-hextra | demo-asciinema</concerns>
<practices>Define the site's target audience and what content they need · Theme version: Hextra v0.12.1 — pinned in website/go.mod · Identify which workflows need demo reels — prioritize the "first 5 minutes" experience · Current demos: helix-quickstart (full lifecycle), helix-concerns (drift detection), helix-evolve (requirement threading)</practices>
<governing>scripts/refresh_context_digests.py — def build_digest( item: dict, principles: list[str], library: dict[str, dict[str, list[str]]], root: Path, ) -> tuple[str, list[str]]: existing_digest, body = split_digest(item.get("description", "")) labels = infer_area_labels(item) bead_areas = {label.removeprefix("area:") for label in labels} matched = [ name for name, data in library.items() if concern_matches(bead_areas, data["areas"]) ] adrs = build_adrs(root, item, labels, matched, library) practices = [] for name in matched: practices.extend( select_digest_practices( library[name]["library_practices"], library[name]["override_practices"], limit=2, ) ) extra_tags = extract_extra_tags(existing_digest)</governing>
</context-digest>

Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: scripts/refresh_context_digests.py:123
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: The new select_digest_practices() merge heuristic still drops override-specific guidance when the first override bullet duplicates the first library bullet. The function always appends library_practices[0] and override_practices[0], then deduplicates and truncates to limit. With limit=2, a case like library=[A, B], overrides=[A, C] returns [A, B], so the digest again omits all override-only content even though FEAT-006 and the reviewed bead's acceptance require merged practices to include both baseline and override guidance when overrides exist.
Suggested fix: When overrides are present, select at least one non-duplicate override bullet before truncation. Add a regression test covering a duplicated-first-bullet case so the helper cannot silently regress back to library-only output.
notes:
<measure-results>
  <timestamp>2026-04-10T20:06:28Z</timestamp>
  <status>PASS</status>
  <acceptance>
    <criterion name='duplicate-first-override regression covered' status='pass' evidence='python3 -m unittest discover -s tests -p '"test_refresh_context_digests.py"' ran 4 tests including the duplicated-first-bullet selector case and exited 0.'/>
    <criterion name='override-specific practice preserved' status='pass' evidence='select_digest_practices(["Library practice A", "Library practice B"], ["Library practice A", "Override practice C"], 2) now returns one library practice plus the unique override practice via tests/test_refresh_context_digests.py.'/>
    <criterion name='repo-hygiene' status='pass' evidence='git diff --check exited 0 after the scoped change.'/>
  </acceptance>
  <gates>
    <gate concern='testing' command='python3 -m unittest discover -s tests -p '"test_refresh_context_digests.py"'' status='pass'/>
    <gate concern='repo' command='git diff --check' status='pass'/>
  </gates>
  <ratchets />
</measure-results>

Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: docs/helix/04-build/implementation-plan.md:58
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: The refreshed plan says the current command surface was validated against `bash scripts/helix help`, then lists `input` and groups `commit` as part of that validated surface. But `bash scripts/helix help` does not print an `input` command at all, and its Usage block omits `commit` as an invocable surface even though the dispatcher has `commit)` and `input)` handlers in scripts/helix. That leaves the implementation plan internally inconsistent with the verification source it cites, so operators using the plan cannot tell whether the CLI help is stale or the plan is overstating what the documented command surface actually is.
Suggested fix: Make the implementation plan and `bash scripts/helix help` agree. Either remove commands from the plan until the help text advertises them, or update the help output so every command claimed in the Current Command Surface section is visible in the documented CLI surface.
title: correctness: reconcile implementation-plan command surface with helix help evidence

description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: docs/README.md:16
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: Commit fdc7245 fixed the legacy top-level phase paths, but the repo-specific directory tree still does not match the live `docs/helix` surface in this repository. The tree omits real paths such as `docs/helix/00-discover`, `docs/helix/01-frame/concerns.md`, `docs/helix/02-design/security-architecture.md`, `docs/helix/03-test/test-plans/`, and `docs/helix/03-test/executions/`, while still advertising nonexistent entries such as `docs/helix/01-frame/principles.md`, `docs/helix/02-design/security.md`, `docs/helix/03-test/test-plan.md`, and `docs/helix/03-test/test-procedures.md`. Because the section is introduced as "In this repository", readers are told this synthetic tree is the live repo layout and can still be sent to files that do not exist.
Suggested fix: Update the repo-specific directory tree and any accompanying artifact summaries so they reflect the real `docs/helix` contents of this repository, or explicitly relabel the section as an illustrative generic example instead of a live repo map.
title: correctness: make docs README repo tree match live docs/helix contents

Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: workflows/actions/fresh-eyes-review.md:188
Category: drift
Severity: medium
Description: The fresh-eyes review filing pattern creates follow-on build beads with only helix, phase:build, and review-finding labels. Concern resolution matches area-scoped practices from area:* labels, so review-filed site/cli/workflow findings re-enter the queue without the concern context they need. The reviewed bead helix-64ed217e is a live example: it fixed a site Playwright issue but was created without area:site, which meant e2e-playwright could not be matched automatically from tracker metadata.
Suggested fix: Update the review action so filing a finding preserves or derives the relevant area:* labels from the reviewed scope or governing bead, and document that requirement wherever bead creation examples appear.
title: drift: preserve area labels on review-filed findings

acceptance: Across workflows/phases/05-deploy/artifacts/{deployment-checklist,monitoring-setup,runbook}/meta.yml, every `dependencies.requires[].path` that references another 05-deploy artifact points to an artifact with `workflow.creation_order` less than or equal to the current artifact, and no cycle remains among those three deploy artifacts.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: workflows/phases/05-deploy/artifacts/deployment-checklist/meta.yml:19 and workflows/phases/05-deploy/artifacts/monitoring-setup/meta.yml:23
Category: integration
Severity: medium
Description: Commit 82cf344 removes the runbook's backward outputs, but the 05-deploy graph is still not order-consistent. `deployment-checklist` declares `creation_order: 1` while requiring `monitoring-setup` and `runbook`, and `monitoring-setup` declares `creation_order: 2` while requiring `runbook`. Automation that treats `dependencies.requires` as upstream prerequisites still sees forward edges to later artifacts, so the deploy graph remains cyclic or unschedulable despite the closed bead claiming consistency.
Suggested fix: Reconcile `creation_order` and `dependencies.requires` across deployment-checklist, monitoring-setup, and runbook so every deploy-phase prerequisite points only to an earlier-or-equal artifact, or move those references into non-prerequisite relationship fields if they are informational only.
notes:

    <criterion name='no cycle remains among deployment-checklist, monitoring-setup, and runbook' status='pass' evidence='python3 zero-dependency structural check reported orders {deployment-checklist:1, monitoring-setup:2, runbook:3}, bad_edges=[], cycle=False'/>
  </acceptance>
  <gates>

description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: scripts/refresh_context_digests.py:205
Category: drift
Severity: medium
Description: The refreshed workflow docs now tell triage, evolve, polish, and context-digest assembly to use the repo helper when it exists, but the helper does not implement the required ADR and governing-spec phases of the FEAT-006 assembly algorithm. `build_digest()` never resolves ADR references at all, and `build_governing()` reduces governing context to `spec-id` plus the first acceptance clause instead of loading the referenced artifact and extracting the governing requirement. The live digest on helix-7b3c6980 shows the result: no <adrs> block and only `FEAT-006 — Open beads in scope...` as governing context. That leaves future beads with materially less authority than the workflow contract promises while the docs advertise the helper as the canonical deterministic path.
Suggested fix: Either extend the helper to resolve ADR references and governing clauses from the referenced spec files, or narrow the workflow docs so the helper is explicitly limited to label refresh/basic digest normalization until it implements the full FEAT-006 assembly contract.
title: drift: make digest helper honor ADR and governing-spec assembly requirements

description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: tests/validate-skills.sh:255
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: The new flagged-fixture check does not actually prove the non-measurable acceptance was flagged for this rule. bash tests/validate-skills.sh stays green even though the flagged fixture invocation prints "validated measurable acceptance on 0 execution-ready bead(s)", because the helper skips execution-eligible=false beads and the test asserts neither a checked count nor a reason tied to non-measurable acceptance. A future regression could change the fixture from "flagged for vague acceptance" to any generic non-executable state and this lane would still pass.
Suggested fix: Make the validation assert the flagged path explicitly, for example by checking for a diagnostic or fixture field that records the bead was marked not execution-ready because of non-measurable acceptance, and fail if the helper reports zero relevant beads without that evidence.

acceptance: Story-level state detection keeps a story in DEPLOY while any matching `phase:deploy` bead is not closed, including `status: in_progress`; workflows/state-rules.yml, workflows/state-machine.yaml, and tests/validate-state-rules.sh all state that rule deterministically; git diff --check passes.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: workflows/state-rules.yml:46
Category: integration
Severity: medium
Description: Commit 27c7051 makes ITERATE require all matching deploy beads to be complete and no open deploy bead to remain, but the surrounding story-state contract still treats active deploy work as `open` only. HELIX claims execution beads by setting `status: in_progress` (docs/helix/02-design/technical-designs/TD-002-helix-cli.md:173-179, workflows/EXECUTION.md:457, tests/helix-cli.sh:2364). A story with a claimed deploy bead therefore no longer satisfies the updated ITERATE condition, yet the written DEPLOY examples and query hints still talk only about open deploy issues. That leaves the contract without a deterministic phase for stories whose deploy work is actively in progress.
Suggested fix: Update the deploy/iterate state-detection contract so any non-closed matching `phase:deploy` bead, including `in_progress`, keeps the story in DEPLOY. Align workflows/state-rules.yml, workflows/state-machine.yaml, workflows/artifact-hierarchy.md, and tests/validate-state-rules.sh around that tracker-status rule.

acceptance: The HELIX iterate contract defines a deterministic way to prove that story `US-{id}` reached ITERATE. Either canonical iterate output templates include an explicit story-reference field with documented query rules, or the story state machine stops requiring shared iterate docs to reference `US-{id}` and instead uses deploy/tracker evidence that already exists.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: workflows/state-machine.yaml:224
Category: integration
Severity: medium
Description: The new contract says a story reaches ITERATE when shared iterate outputs or linked follow-on work reference `US-{id}`, but the canonical iterate artifacts do not define any required story identifier or story-reference field. `metrics-dashboard`, `security-metrics`, and `improvement-backlog` are iteration-wide documents, and their templates currently expose review windows, metrics, recommendations, and tracker refs without any story-scoped key. As written, the new state machine requires story-linked iterate evidence that the canonical iterate outputs are not required to carry, so story-level iterate detection becomes non-deterministic whenever a deploy completes without separate follow-on issues.
Suggested fix: Either add an explicit story-reference contract to the iterate outputs/templates and document how it is queried, or keep story-level iterate detection anchored in deploy/tracker evidence until a deterministic story-keyed iterate artifact exists.
notes:

  </acceptance>
  <gates>

</measure-results>
title: integration: define story-keyed iterate evidence before retiring IR state detection

acceptance: ddx bead show helix-8093c918 reports closing_commit_sha=d242771dbfcbb8baf7585f6c49f049b5aad30f13, and automated last-commit review resolution for that bead targets the queue-snapshot implementation commit rather than the unrelated parent tracker-close commit.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: .ddx/beads.jsonl:99
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: Commit d242771 closes bead helix-8093c918 in .ddx/beads.jsonl, but the row records closing_commit_sha=5122af50a8015de1f340f84a5965f79f5ba8cf31, which is the unrelated parent commit that closed helix-420f4f76. That leaves the tracker pointing at the wrong implementation diff for helix-8093c918. In the automated review loop, fresh-eyes review prefers a bead's closing_commit_sha when resolving the implementation commit behind tracker-only follow-up commits, so this bad SHA can send later review or archaeology to an unrelated change and break durable evidence for what actually fixed the queue snapshot.
Suggested fix: Update helix-8093c918 so closing_commit_sha records the actual implementation commit d242771dbfcbb8baf7585f6c49f049b5aad30f13, and tighten the closure bookkeeping path so a bead closed in the current commit cannot inherit an unrelated parent SHA.

acceptance: A deterministic repo test fails on a checked-in vague execution-ready acceptance fixture, passes when the fixture is flagged or rejected as not execution-ready, and runs from the standard validation lane together with bash tests/validate-skills.sh
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: tests/validate-skills.sh:207
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: The implementation acceptance requires deterministic validation of at least one rejection or flagging path for non-measurable acceptance text, but the new validator only checks for literal substrings in docs and skills. `bash tests/validate-skills.sh` passes as long as the wording remains present, even if `helix-triage` or `helix-polish` never actually reject or reroute a vague execution-ready bead. A regression in the real queue-readiness behavior would therefore ship with a green validation lane because the test never exercises any negative case.
Suggested fix: Add a deterministic fixture-based validation that feeds vague acceptance text such as `works correctly` through the canonical triage/polish validation surface, or a dedicated helper used by those surfaces, and assert that the bead is flagged not execution-ready or rejected.
notes:
<measure-results>

    <criterion name='flagged non-execution-ready fixture passes' status='pass' evidence='python3 scripts/validate_execution_ready_beads.py tests/fixtures/execution-ready-beads/flagged-acceptance.jsonl exits 0.'/>
    <criterion name='standard validation lane covers the behavior check' status='pass' evidence='bash tests/validate-skills.sh passes and now runs the helper against both checked-in fixtures.'/>

description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: scripts/refresh_context_digests.py:110
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: The new digest helper discards library practices whenever a concern has project overrides. `build_concern_library()` uses `overrides.get(name) or parse_practice_bullets(...)`, so every matched concern in this repo contributes only override bullets to the rebuilt digest. FEAT-006 and the context-digest reference require project overrides to be applied on top of library practices, not to replace them entirely. The resulting live digests omit base concern guidance such as library testing and artifact-sync practices, which weakens the self-contained execution context the helper was introduced to restore.
Suggested fix: Merge library practices with project overrides deterministically instead of choosing one or the other. Preserve the core area-matched library practices, then layer repo-specific override bullets on top when assembling the compact digest.

<governing>workflows/phases/05-deploy/artifacts/runbook/meta.yml — A deterministic validation command in the repo fails if a 05-deploy artifact requires a later artifact or if the deploy artifact graph contains a cycle, and the check is wired into the standard validation lane used for workflow-contract changes.</governing>

Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: workflows/phases/05-deploy/artifacts/runbook/meta.yml:11
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: The implementation bead for commit 82cf344 was measured with one-file `yq` checks against `runbook/meta.yml`, and the repo has no deterministic validation that checks cross-artifact creation-order consistency for the 05-deploy graph. That gap let the fix pass even though `deployment-checklist` and `monitoring-setup` still require later deploy artifacts. Without a shared validator, future graph edits can again report local success while leaving the canonical deploy contract contradictory.
Suggested fix: Add a deterministic repo validation that loads the 05-deploy artifact metadata together and fails when any `dependencies.requires` edge points to a later artifact or when the deploy artifact graph contains a cycle. Run it in the normal workflow-contract validation lane.

acceptance: `workflows/phases/05-deploy/artifacts/runbook/meta.yml` no longer declares `deployment-checklist` or `monitoring-setup` as enabled outputs, and its relationship metadata is consistent with `creation_order: 3` and the listed `requires` dependencies.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: workflows/phases/05-deploy/artifacts/runbook/meta.yml:33
Category: integration
Severity: medium
Description: The restored runbook metadata says the artifact requires `deployment-checklist` and `monitoring-setup`, but it also declares those same peer artifacts as outputs in `dependencies.enables` and `relationships.informs` while `creation_order` is 3. That makes the artifact graph internally contradictory: automation reading `enables` can treat earlier deploy artifacts as newly unlocked after runbook creation even though the runbook itself depends on them.
Suggested fix: Remove backward `enables` and downstream relationship claims for earlier deploy artifacts, or otherwise rewrite the runbook/checklist/monitoring metadata so the dependency graph and creation order are acyclic and consistent.

Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: docs/helix/04-build/implementation-plan.md:82
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: The Live Queue Snapshot section says the snapshot was captured after claiming `helix-62559453` and that there was `1` in-progress open bead (`helix-62559453`). But the backlog tables immediately below enumerate 22 issue IDs that exactly match the current post-close open queue and do not include `helix-62559453` at all. The section therefore mixes two different tracker states: a snapshot that still counts the refresh bead as open, and a backlog listing that already excludes it. Because this document is meant to steer implementation ordering from live tracker state, that mismatch can send later runs down the wrong queue or make the counts impossible to audit against `ddx bead status` / `ddx bead list --status open --json`.
Suggested fix: Rebuild the Live Queue Snapshot and backlog tables from one tracker moment and make them agree. If `helix-62559453` is part of the counted open queue, include it or reduce the backlog table count accordingly; otherwise rewrite the prose/counts to the post-close queue state and remove the in-progress claim.

acceptance: HELIX design/docs define an integration contract where HELIX selects stage tier and harness constraints while DDx resolves the concrete model via ddx-agent catalog policy; compatibility for existing HELIX smart/cheap overrides is specified; scripts/helix and tests/helix-cli.sh implementation steps are identified without leaving hardcoded model-version policy in HELIX.
description:
<context-digest>
<principles>Design for Change · Design for Simplicity · Validate Your Work · Make Intent Explicit · Prefer Reversible Decisions</principles>
<concerns>hugo-hextra | demo-asciinema</concerns>
<practices>Theme version: Hextra v0.12.1 — pinned in website/go.mod · Hugo version: 0.159.2 extended — pinned in .github/workflows/pages.yml · Current demos: helix-quickstart (full lifecycle), helix-concerns (drift detection), helix-evolve (requirement threading) · Agent harness: ddx agent run --harness claude — not claude -p</practices>
<governing>workflows/EXECUTION.md — HELIX design/docs define an integration contract where HELIX selects stage tier and harness constraints while DDx resolves the concrete model via ddx-agent catalog policy</governing>
<evidence>
- scripts/helix currently hardcodes concrete smart/cheap model names in resolve_model(): claude-opus-4-6, claude-sonnet-4-6, gpt-5.4, gpt-5.4-mini.
- HELIX docs and help text expose --smart-model / --cheap-model and describe concrete defaults in ways that duplicate DDx policy.
- The desired behavior is: HELIX decides whether a stage needs smart/fast/cheap and which harness family to use; DDx resolves the concrete current model through ddx-agent catalog policy under user constraints.
- This removes split-brain model policy and avoids HELIX drifting behind current recommended models when ddx-agent catalog data changes.
</evidence>
</context-digest>

Design how HELIX should consume DDx tier/model policy backed by ddx-agent's shared model catalog instead of hardcoding concrete model names.

Scope:
- Define the integration contract between HELIX and DDx for tier-based model selection.
- Decide whether HELIX should pass tier names, family aliases, harness constraints, or another structured request to DDx.
- Identify compatibility behavior for existing HELIX_SMART_MODEL / HELIX_CHEAP_MODEL overrides and existing CLI flags.
- Update HELIX execution contract/docs so supervisory stage selection remains in HELIX while concrete model policy is delegated downward through DDx to ddx-agent catalog data.
- Outline the implementation/test plan for scripts/helix and tests/helix-cli.sh.

Notes:
- Keep the responsibility split crisp: HELIX owns stage policy (review/design/align vs build/check/polish), DDx owns concrete harness/model resolution, ddx-agent owns reusable model catalog data.
- The resulting design should avoid reintroducing hardcoded codex/claude model version strings in HELIX.

title: Make HELIX consume DDx tier policy backed by ddx-agent model catalog

description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: scripts/validate_execution_ready_beads.py:59
Category: integration
Severity: medium
Description: The new validator's execution-ready predicate does not match the canonical queue surface. On the live tracker, running python3 scripts/validate_execution_ready_beads.py .ddx/beads.jsonl inspects 193 beads, including 169 closed beads, while ddx bead ready --execution --json reports 28 execution-ready beads. Because the helper ignores status and queue-readiness semantics, its default path produces noisy failures against historical tracker state instead of validating the same bead set HELIX would actually execute.
Suggested fix: Derive the candidate bead set from ddx bead ready --execution --json or replicate its readiness filters exactly before applying acceptance validation, and add a deterministic fixture that proves closed, deferred, and otherwise non-ready beads are excluded.
title: integration: align execution-ready validator with ddx ready semantics

acceptance: In an automated run-loop cycle that creates a tracker-closure sync commit after implementation, the post-implementation review threshold and review scope still inspect the implementation diff rather than the closure-sync diff; deterministic tests cover the commit-selection behavior and pass.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: scripts/helix:2936
Category: integration
Severity: high
Description: In the automated run loop, post-implementation review decides whether to run and what to review from `git diff HEAD~1`, then invokes `run_review last-commit`. In this repository the most recent commit after the implementation cycle is `91860b8` (`helix-c0d6bdf3 sync tracker closure`), which changes only `.ddx/beads.jsonl`. The actual implementation diff is one commit earlier at `d6da2d5`. This means the auto-review threshold can undercount the work as a tiny tracker-only change and skip review, or review only bookkeeping instead of the implementation that just ran. The action contract at `workflows/actions/fresh-eyes-review.md:39` and the operator docs at `workflows/EXECUTION.md:604` reinforce the same incorrect `last-commit` assumption for this in-loop context.
Suggested fix: Thread the executed issue ID or implementation commit SHA through the run loop and make auto-review inspect that implementation diff instead of raw HEAD~1 when a tracker close sync commit exists. Update the fresh-eyes review action and execution docs to describe the in-loop behavior explicitly, and add deterministic coverage showing review still targets the implementation commit after a tracker-closure sync commit.
title: integration: review last-commit should target implementation diff, not tracker closure sync

<governing>HELIX should not force DDX_BEAD_PREFIX=hx inside scripts/helix. DDx already derives the correct bead prefix from repo configuration or repo name, and overriding it causes HELIX to look at the wrong issue namespace inside the helix repo.</governing>

- helix run is selecting hx-prefixed historical issues instead of the current helix-prefixed FEAT-011 queue.

Remove the hard-coded DDX_BEAD_PREFIX export so HELIX reads the current repo's real tracker namespace.

- add deterministic coverage that helix reads the helix-prefixed queue in this repo instead of the historical hx-prefixed namespace
- re-run the relevant wrapper tests and restart the Claude worker against the corrected queue
title: Fix HELIX tracker prefix override so run loop sees helix-prefixed queue

description:
<context-digest>
<principles>Design for Change · Design for Simplicity · Validate Your Work · Make Intent Explicit · Prefer Reversible Decisions</principles>
<concerns>hugo-hextra | demo-asciinema | e2e-playwright</concerns>
<practices>Theme version: Hextra v0.12.1 — pinned in website/go.mod · Hugo version: 0.159.2 extended — pinned in .github/workflows/pages.yml · Current demos: helix-quickstart (full lifecycle), helix-concerns (drift detection), helix-evolve (requirement threading) · Agent harness: ddx agent run --harness claude — not claude -p · Test location: website/e2e/microsite.spec.ts · Config: website/playwright.config.ts with video on, trace on failure</practices>
<governing>website/assets/json/search-data.json — After building the site in website/, the rendered search index contains /docs/glossary/ but not /glossary, and selecting the glossary search result navigates to an existing page.</governing>
</context-digest>

Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: website/assets/json/search-data.json:35
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: The new repo-owned search-data override preserves a broken synthetic glossary route. After running the Hugo build in website/, the rendered public/en.search-data.json contains both /docs/glossary/ and a second /glossary record, even though the actual page lives only at /docs/glossary/. Hextra's search client uses those JSON keys directly as result routes, so searching for glossary content can surface a non-existent destination. This is now HELIX-owned behavior because commit e61a4f6 shadows the upstream template into website/assets/json/search-data.json.
Suggested fix: Build the synthetic glossary record from the real glossary page permalink instead of relLangURL "glossary", and add a deterministic assertion that the rendered search index contains no nonexistent glossary route.
notes:
<measure-results>
  <timestamp>2026-04-10T19:05:00Z</timestamp>
  <status>PASS</status>
  <acceptance>
    <criterion name='rendered glossary route' status='pass' evidence='cd website && hugo --gc --minify succeeds, and website/public/en.search-data.json contains a glossary index key ending in /docs/glossary/ with no sibling key ending only in /glossary/.'/>
    <criterion name='search result navigates to a live page' status='pass' evidence='cd website && npx playwright test e2e/microsite.spec.ts passes 17 tests, including Search Workflows coverage that opens site search, queries glossary content, and navigates to the glossary page.'/>
  </acceptance>
  <gates>
    <gate concern='hugo-hextra' command='cd website && hugo --gc --minify' status='pass'/>
    <gate concern='e2e-playwright' command='cd website && npx playwright test e2e/microsite.spec.ts' status='pass'/>
    <gate concern='repo-hygiene' command='git diff --check' status='pass'/>
  </gates>
  <notes>The search-data template already resolves glossary entries through the real glossary page permalink on the current branch; this pass adds a deterministic Playwright assertion against the rendered search index so the broken synthetic route cannot regress silently.</notes>
</measure-results>
title: correctness: search index emits broken glossary route

description:

File: skills/helix-triage/SKILL.md:51
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: The revised triage skill now tells agents to write deterministic acceptance and says queue-drained execution beads should prefer exact commands, files, and end-state conditions, but it still does not define the same negative path that polish now has when the governing artifacts do not support a measurable execution contract. workflows/EXECUTION.md:606-608 says beads that cannot be closed from explicit evidence should be refined by helix polish or helix triage before entering the execution queue. As written, helix-triage can still create phase:build beads with vague acceptance text whenever the intake request is underspecified, leaving execution readiness to later human judgment instead of blocking it at creation time.
Suggested fix: Extend skills/helix-triage/SKILL.md so that when deterministic success criteria cannot be derived, triage routes the work back to planning/polish or explicitly marks it not execution-ready instead of creating a queue-ready implementation bead.
title: correctness: triage still lacks a non-execution-ready fallback for vague build beads

acceptance: python3 -m unittest discover -s tests -p 'test_refresh_context_digests.py' includes a regression case where select_digest_practices(['Library practice A', 'Library practice B'], ['Library practice B', 'Override practice C'], 2) returns ['Library practice A', 'Override practice C']; git diff --check passes.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: scripts/refresh_context_digests.py:379
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: The updated select_digest_practices() helper only avoids duplicates against the already-prioritized prefix, so it still drops override-specific guidance when an early override duplicates a later library bullet. For example, select_digest_practices(['Library practice A', 'Library practice B'], ['Library practice B', 'Override practice C'], 2) currently returns ['Library practice A', 'Library practice B'] instead of preserving any override-only practice. That still violates the merged-practices contract for context digests when overrides exist.
Suggested fix: Choose the prioritized override from override bullets that are not present anywhere in library_practices before falling back to a duplicate, and add a regression test for the later-library-duplicate case so the digest always retains one override-specific practice when available.
title: correctness: keep override-specific digest practice when an early override duplicates any library practice

description:

<concerns>cli | workflow | ddx-boundary</concerns>

Simplify HELIX agent dispatch so `scripts/helix` delegates default model selection and harness management to DDx instead of re-implementing them locally.

Scope:
- remove HELIX default concrete model selection from normal ddx-agent dispatch paths
- resolve the embedded DDx agent aliases (`ddx-agent`, `embedded`, legacy `forge`) against the live `ddx agent list` surface so HELIX works across DDx rename states
- keep explicit user overrides (`--model`, `HELIX_SMART_MODEL`, `HELIX_CHEAP_MODEL`) working as overrides, not defaults
- add deterministic CLI tests covering alias resolution and absence of implicit `--model` flags for default dispatch
- if DDx is missing cleaner routing surfaces such as `--profile`, file a DDx bead instead of rebuilding that logic in HELIX
title: Delegate HELIX agent selection to DDx and resolve embedded-harness aliases

description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: docs/README.md:210
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: The "Using Helix Phases in Projects" section still prescribes a `docs/helix-phases/` layout and `ddx apply workflows/helix/phases/...` commands, but this repository and the active workflow contract use `docs/helix/` and `workflows/phases/...`. The example structure and template commands therefore point to directories that do not exist, so a user following the README will fail to locate the documented paths or run the documented commands.
Suggested fix: Update the project-convention section to describe the supported `docs/helix/` layout and valid `workflows/phases/...` artifact paths or replace the commands with the current supported workflow entrypoints.
title: correctness: replace stale helix-phases and ddx apply paths in docs README

acceptance: With DDX_BEAD_DIR pointing outside the Git worktree, `bash scripts/helix commit <issue>` exits successfully, the bead is closed with closing_commit_sha equal to the implementation commit, and the existing in-repo tracker-sync commit behavior remains covered by deterministic tests.
description:
<context-digest>
<principles>Design for Change · Design for Simplicity · Validate Your Work · Make Intent Explicit · Prefer Reversible Decisions</principles>
<concerns>hugo-hextra | demo-asciinema</concerns>
<practices>Define the site's target audience and what content they need · Theme version: Hextra v0.12.1 — pinned in website/go.mod · Identify which workflows need demo reels — prioritize the "first 5 minutes" experience · Current demos: helix-quickstart (full lifecycle), helix-concerns (drift detection), helix-evolve (requirement threading)</practices>
<governing>FEAT-005 — implementation_prompt() { local selector="${1:-}" local context_file="${target_root}/.helix/context.md" cat <<EOF $(if [[ -f "$context_file" ]]; then cat "$context_file"; printf '\n---\n\n'; fi) Execute the following HELIX implementation action. $(inline_action "${helix_docs_root}/actions/implementation.md") Use supporting references at ${helix_docs_root}/README.md and ${helix_docs_root}/phases/04-build/enforcer.md as needed. Execute in the current repository rooted at ${target_root}. Issue tracker: built-in JSONL at ${tracker_file} (legacy: .ddx/issues.jsonl). Use \ddx bead ready --json\, \ddx bead show <id>\, \ddx bead update <id> --claim\, \ddx bead close <id>\. Concurrency rule: re-read the selected issue immediately before claim and immediately before close. If tracker metadata drifted materially, do not claim or close from stale assumptions; re-check instead. ${selector:+Implementation target: ${selector}.} CRITICAL: If tests fail after your changes, fix them. 99% of test failures are caused by your changes, not by pre-existing breakage. Do not blame external causes, do not skip tests, do not commit with failing tests. Fix the code or fix the test. Retry up to 3 times before giving up. Never commit broken work. Follow the action exactly. Do the work — do not just describe what you would do. Session log: ${helix_log} EOF }</governing>
</context-digest>

Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: scripts/helix:2388
Category: integration
Severity: medium
Description: Commit ae4c544 unconditionally runs `git add "$tracker_file" && git commit ...` after `tracker_close`. That works when the built-in tracker lives under the repo, but HELIX still accepts `DDX_BEAD_DIR` / `HELIX_TRACKER_DIR` overrides. When the tracker directory is external, `git add` fails with `is outside repository` after the implementation commit has already been created. Reproduction: in a temp repo with `DDX_BEAD_DIR` pointing at a sibling directory, `bash scripts/helix commit hx-ext-0` exits 128 after creating the implementation commit, leaves the bead closed only in the external tracker file, and never reaches push/reporting.
Suggested fix: Gate the tracker-sync commit on whether `$tracker_file` is inside `$target_root`. If the tracker is external, still sync `closing_commit_sha` in the tracker, but skip the repo-local tracker commit or use a different persistence path that does not require `git add` on an out-of-repo file. Add a deterministic harness test covering external `DDX_BEAD_DIR` mode.
title: integration: keep helix commit working with external tracker dirs

description:
Sparse intake request: "design a CRM".

Interpretation:
- Core intent: create the governed planning/design work needed for a new CRM product.
- Artifact layer affected: frame and design, with downstream decomposition into execution-ready work.
- Explicit constraints: none beyond high autonomy.

Current repo search found no existing CRM artifact stack or matching open bead. The HELIX repo's governing PRD is about HELIX itself, so a separate escalation bead records the repo-scope conflict. Until that is resolved, treat this as greenfield CRM planning work managed through HELIX.

Working assumptions for downstream beads:
- B2B web CRM for a small-to-mid-size sales team
- MVP includes contacts, accounts, leads/opportunities, tasks/activities, notes, pipeline visibility, and basic reporting
- Multi-user SaaS posture; implementation architecture still to be designed
title: Design a CRM

acceptance:
1. The user explicitly chooses whether the CRM work should live in a separate project/repository, in HELIX as an example/demo artifact set, or be reinterpreted as HELIX product work.
2. The chosen target identifies where governing frame artifacts should live.
3. Follow-on planning beads can then be created without contradicting `docs/helix/01-frame/prd.md` or `workflows/README.md`.
description:
Sparse intake request: "design a CRM".

Interpretation at autonomy=medium: the user wants HELIX to seed governed planning work for a new CRM product design effort.

Conflict detected during artifact traversal: this repository's higher-authority frame artifacts define HELIX itself as the product (`docs/helix/01-frame/prd.md`, `workflows/README.md`). Creating CRM vision/PRD/design artifacts directly in this repo would therefore contradict the current product scope unless the request is intended as one of the following:
- a separate CRM project/repository that should use HELIX
- a HELIX example/demo artifact pack about CRM design
- a reinterpretation into HELIX product work rather than a CRM product

Deterministic next step: obtain the target-project decision before creating downstream CRM frame or design beads.
notes: Conflict evidence: `docs/helix/01-frame/prd.md` states HELIX is a development control system for AI-assisted software delivery, and `workflows/README.md` defines this repository as the HELIX workflow contract. CRM product artifacts would need a user-confirmed target before downstream frame/design work can be created non-conflictingly at medium autonomy.
title: Clarify CRM intake target before creating frame/design artifacts

acceptance: Run helix run --agent claude --summary --max-cycles 3 against ddx repo with 3 ready beads. After completion: all 3 beads remain closed. No bead reverts to open. Blocker report shows 0 intractable issues.
description: helix run closes beads via the agent, but between cycles beads revert from closed to open. The ddx bead JSONL store locking is sound (chaos tests prove it). The bug is in scripts/helix run loop: after the agent closes a bead and commits, the next cycle startup reclaims or reopens it. Likely cause: the run loop reads beads.jsonl, caches bead state, dispatches agent, then writes back the stale cached state — clobbering the agent's close. Fix: re-read beads.jsonl fresh after each agent call returns. Never cache bead state across cycle boundaries.
title: Fix helix run bead-state-drift: closed beads revert to open between cycles

acceptance: condense_codex_output() removed from scripts/helix. Codex output filtering delegated to ddx exec run. All existing helix-cli.sh tests pass. No regression in summary-mode output quality.
description:
condense_codex_output() in scripts/helix (lines ~525-620) is a bash reimplementation of output filtering that belongs in DDx's agent service (ddx-205af2f4). Once DDx implements the output condensing filter in ddx exec run, HELIX should:

1. Remove condense_codex_output() from scripts/helix entirely
2. Rely on ddx exec run's condensed output instead of filtering in bash
3. Update run_agent_prompt() codex branch to consume DDx-filtered output
4. Update tests that depend on condense_codex_output behavior

The current bash filter keeps: helix: progress lines, $ tool calls, errors, issue IDs, markdown headings/tables, phase markers. Drops: raw diffs, codex boilerplate, bulk verbose output. This logic should move to DDx.

Depends on: ddx-205af2f4 (DDx agent output condensing filter)
title: Port condense_codex_output to DDx agent output filter and remove from helix

Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: workflows/state-rules.yml:42
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: The new story-level iterate artifact pattern is the shared directory `docs/helix/06-iterate/`. Under the same file, phase detection still uses `highest-artifact`, so once the iterate directory exists the rule can satisfy the iterate phase for every story regardless of whether that story has any iterate evidence. This collapses per-story state detection and can make partially completed stories appear to have reached ITERATE simply because the project has shared iterate docs.
Suggested fix: Remove the static iterate directory pattern from story-level state detection and make iterate determination query-only, or introduce a story-keyed evidence pattern that cannot match unrelated stories.

acceptance: The story-level iterate contract handles multiple phase:deploy issues deterministically. Either HELIX documents one canonical deploy bead per story, or the state rule/query explicitly requires all matching deploy issues closed and zero open deploy issues before a story enters ITERATE.
description:
<context-digest>
<principles>Design for Change · Design for Simplicity · Validate Your Work · Make Intent Explicit · Prefer Reversible Decisions</principles>
<concerns>hugo-hextra | demo-asciinema</concerns>
<practices>Define the site's target audience and what content they need · Theme version: Hextra v0.12.1 — pinned in website/go.mod · Identify which workflows need demo reels — prioritize the "first 5 minutes" experience · Current demos: helix-quickstart (full lifecycle), helix-concerns (drift detection), helix-evolve (requirement threading)</practices>
<governing>workflows/state-rules.yml — iterate: type: iterate-evidence phase: iterate query_only: true query_command: "Inspect completed tracker issues labeled helix, phase:deploy, story:US-{id}. Treat closure of the story's deploy issue as the deterministic ITERATE threshold. Linked tracker follow-on work is supporting evidence when present; canonical iterate outputs remain shared iteration context and are not queried by story ID."</governing>
</context-digest>

Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: workflows/state-rules.yml:46
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: The new iterate query says to inspect completed tracker issues labeled `helix, phase:deploy, story:US-{id}` and then treats closure of the story's deploy issue as the deterministic ITERATE threshold. Elsewhere in the same contract, HELIX still allows deploy issue(s) per story (for example `transitions.story.build_to_deploy`, `transitions.story.deploy_to_iterate`, `workflows/state-machine.yaml`, and `workflows/artifact-hierarchy.md` all use plural `issue(s)`). Without an explicit rule that no open `phase:deploy` issues may remain, the story can be classified as ITERATE as soon as one deploy bead closes even if another deploy bead for the same story is still open.
Suggested fix: Define whether a story has exactly one canonical deploy bead or, if multiple deploy beads are allowed, update the iterate query/rule to require all matching `phase:deploy` issues to be closed and no open deploy issue to remain before the story is in ITERATE.
notes:
<measure-results>
  <timestamp>2026-04-10T17:49:27Z</timestamp>
  <status>PASS</status>
  <acceptance>
    <criterion name='iterate-query-requires-all-deploy-issues-closed' status='pass' evidence='workflows/state-rules.yml now treats ITERATE as reachable only when all matching phase:deploy issues are complete and no open deploy issue remains.'/>
    <criterion name='story-level-contract-is-consistent-across-docs' status='pass' evidence='workflows/state-machine.yaml, workflows/artifact-hierarchy.md, and workflows/phases/06-iterate/README.md now use the same multi-deploy threshold wording.'/>
    <criterion name='validator-catches-regression' status='pass' evidence='tests/validate-state-rules.sh now asserts the iterate query inspects deploy issues by story label and requires all matching deploy issues complete with no open deploy issue remaining.'/>
  </acceptance>
  <gates>
    <gate concern='repo' command='bash tests/validate-state-rules.sh' status='pass'/>
    <gate concern='repo' command='git diff --check' status='pass'/>
  </gates>
  <ratchets />
</measure-results>
title: correctness: make deploy-to-iterate detection unambiguous when multiple deploy beads exist

<governing>website/e2e/microsite.spec.ts — The microsite Playwright suite includes a search-focused test that opens the search UI, queries glossary and CLI content, and proves selected search results navigate to existing pages</governing>
</context-digest>

Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: website/e2e/microsite.spec.ts:1
Category: drift
Severity: medium
Description: The FEAT-007 verification lane is green, but the Playwright suite still does not exercise the user-facing search workflow at all. `rg` over `website/e2e/microsite.spec.ts` shows page-load, navigation, and screenshot coverage only; there are no search input interactions or assertions on result routing. That violates the active `e2e-playwright` concern practice that every user-facing workflow have at least one end-to-end test, and it is why the broken `/glossary` search route in the rendered index was able to ship undetected in commit e61a4f6.
Suggested fix: Add a Playwright scenario that opens site search, queries at least glossary and CLI content, asserts the result URLs are valid, and verifies navigation succeeds when a result is selected.

Reproduction:
1. Run `bash tests/helix-cli.sh` in the HELIX repo.
2. Observe the suite pass most cases, then stall in a temp workspace on `bash /Users/erik/Projects/helix/scripts/helix build --ddx-agent --dry-run`.
3. Process tree shows a child `bash .../bin/ddx agent list` still running while the suite is otherwise quiescent.

This blocked full required validation for workflow-contract changes landed under `helix-9b6c9807`.

acceptance:
1. helix input "design a CRM" --autonomy low dispatches a prompt containing Autonomy level: low.
2. helix input --autonomy high "design a CRM" still dispatches Autonomy level: high.
3. Deterministic coverage in tests/helix-cli.sh exercises both flag positions and invalid autonomy.
4. bash tests/helix-cli.sh and git diff --check pass.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: scripts/helix:3395
Category: correctness
Severity: high
Description: The input subcommand parser only consumes --autonomy while it appears before the request. The published contract documents the request-first form (helix input "..." --autonomy high), but the current parser breaks on the first non-flag token and silently falls back to medium. Reproduction against the current branch shows bash scripts/helix input "design a CRM" --autonomy low dispatches a prompt containing Autonomy level: medium, which violates FEAT-011 / TD-011 semantics and can skip the confirmations required by low autonomy. The new deterministic coverage in tests/helix-cli.sh only exercises the unsupported flag-first form, so this documented CLI path remains broken and untested.
Suggested fix: Parse --autonomy in both positions accepted by the documented CLI surface, reject ambiguous argument layouts, and add deterministic tests that cover both helix input --autonomy high "..." and helix input "..." --autonomy low.
notes:

    <criterion name='flag-first autonomy syntax' status='pass' evidence='bash tests/helix-cli.sh covers helix input --autonomy high "design a CRM" -> Autonomy level: high'/>
    <criterion name='invalid and ambiguous input rejection' status='pass' evidence='bash tests/helix-cli.sh covers invalid autonomy, duplicate --autonomy, and ambiguous extra args'/>
    <criterion name='diff hygiene' status='pass' evidence='git diff --check'/>

</measure-results>

description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: tests/validate-skills.sh:6
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: The skill validation script only validates .agents/skills/ symlinks (line 6: package_dir=.agents/skills). It does not validate .claude/skills/ at all. This allowed wrong symlink targets (helix-implement pointing to helix-build, helix-plan pointing to helix-design) and the helix-release asymmetry to persist undetected. Both .agents/skills/ and .claude/skills/ are documented as canonical paths in AGENTS.md.
Suggested fix: Add a second validation pass in validate-skills.sh that checks .claude/skills/ symlinks mirror .agents/skills/ (same targets, same relative form).

Fourth, rewrite the docs so the portable skill surface is clear while deeper HELIX semantics remain in the workflow contract.

description: Fresh-eyes review of commit a06fd30 found two correctness issues in docs/helix/06-iterate/alignment-reviews/AR-2026-03-27-repo.md. First, the queue-health summary records tracker state as '84 total / 8 open / 0 in-progress / 75 closed' without noting the remaining deferred issue, so the published totals are not self-consistent. Second, the report's Execution Order and closing recommendation say the strongest next move is hx-e7324e4f first, even though the same report states hx-c2dd330a is the only execution-safe ready issue. In an automated helix run context that distinction matters: strategic design priority and execution-safe next work are not the same thing. Update the durable report so its queue accounting is mechanically accurate and its next-step guidance distinguishes strategic ordering from the actual execution-safe queue.
design: Keep the alignment report durable and operationally safe. Either make the queue summary exhaustive, or explicitly state which statuses are omitted. Separate strategic follow-up ordering from execution-safe next work so the report does not imply that helix run should select a non-execution design issue ahead of the only execution-safe ready item.

acceptance: After a failed or timed-out implementation cycle, helix run does not retry on top of stale issue-scoped changes. The issue is returned to open before a fresh retry when cleanup succeeds, and the loop stops with a blocker when safe cleanup cannot be proven. Deterministic tests cover rc=1 and timeout cleanup paths, and bash tests/helix-cli.sh plus git diff --check pass.
description: After an implementation cycle fails with rc=1 or a watchdog timeout, the run loop currently retries against whatever partial state the failed attempt left behind. Production runs show this causes repeated timeouts and infinite retry loops because the issue stays claimed and the worktree remains dirty from the prior attempt.
design: At the failure boundary, inspect whether issue-scoped changes are left behind. Before retrying, either revert only the attributable partial changes and unclaim the issue, or stop with a blocker if cleanup cannot be done safely. Ensure timeout and non-timeout failures follow the same cleanup contract and add deterministic coverage for stale-claim release plus clean-worktree enforcement.
title: Clean worktree and release stale claims after failed implementation retries

acceptance: bash tests/helix-cli.sh passes with no FAIL lines, including the 'run should refuse stale close after supersession' test case.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: tests/helix-cli.sh
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: The deterministic test 'run should refuse stale close after supersession' fails with 'expected: open / actual: closed'. The failure is pre-existing (present at HEAD~1 as well as HEAD) and is not caused by commit 8c9fe95. The test expects an issue to remain open after supersession but the run loop closes it. Either the test expectation is stale or the supersession guard in the run loop has a bug.
Suggested fix: Investigate the test at the relevant section of tests/helix-cli.sh. Either fix the supersession guard logic in scripts/helix so it refuses to close superseded issues, or update the test expectation if the current behavior is intentional.
title: correctness: test 'run should refuse stale close after supersession' fails

acceptance: helix status reports persisted lifecycle state including active-run detection, current issue or focused epic, elapsed time, completion rate, recent results, blocker summary, and next recommended action from the run-controller state file. Deterministic coverage proves the status command reads the persisted state safely. bash tests/helix-cli.sh and git diff --check pass.
description: Add 'helix status' command that shows: is a run active (PID file check), current cycle/issue, elapsed time, completion rate, last N cycle results. Read from a state file written by the run loop.
design: Read persisted run-controller state written by helix run and render a structured lifecycle snapshot: active session, current issue or focused epic, elapsed time, completed/attempted counts, recent cycle results, blocker summary, and next recommended action. Fail closed when state is missing or stale rather than guessing.
title: Observability: helix status command for monitoring active runs

acceptance: tests/helix-cli.sh contains a deterministic case where the queue drains and the mocked check command exits non-zero before returning a NEXT_ACTION. In that scenario, helix run fails with the expected queue-drain check error message and does not trip a set -u unbound-variable error. bash tests/helix-cli.sh and git diff --check pass.
description: Fresh-eyes review of commit f43a70d6a8b275d5e041d459a891a3218f4a1770 found that the fix in scripts/helix for an unbound-variable crash after queue-drain check failure is not covered by the deterministic wrapper harness. tests/helix-cli.sh covers parsed NEXT_ACTION outcomes like WAIT and BACKFILL, but it does not simulate the failing path where run_check exits non-zero before the command-substitution assignment completes. Without a regression test, the original set -u failure can return unnoticed in future refactors of the run loop.
design: Add a deterministic wrapper test that forces the mock agent to fail on the queue-drain check path after ready work is exhausted. Assert that helix run --no-auto-review exits non-zero, emits 'helix: check failed after queue drain', and does not surface an unbound-variable error. Keep the test isolated to the queue-drain path so it proves the exact regression fixed by f43a70d6.
title: Add regression coverage for queue-drain check command-substitution failure

description: Replace the current skip-after-2-attempts policy with exponential backoff. When an issue fails to complete, retry with increasing delay and effort before declaring it blocked. If the toolchain doesn't compile, try to fix it. Only give up after escalating effort (e.g. 1 attempt -> retry with more context -> retry with broader scope -> declare blocked). The agent should do hard things, not just pick low-hanging fruit.
title: Run loop: exponential backoff on hard issues instead of immediate bail

acceptance: Repeated implementation attempts against an unchanged non-execution target do not append duplicate or near-duplicate blocker notes to the issue record. description: Fresh-eyes review of commit 2c17f5e737e11ecad1fa8b2c9f47f8da702f5a72 found that the implementation cycle appended another near-duplicate blocker note to issue hx-e7324e4f even though the decisive metadata and queue state were unchanged: the issue remained status=open with labels phase:design/kind:design, execution-eligible=false, and helix tracker ready --json --execution still returned only hx-c2dd330a. Repeated retries against the same non-execution design issue are bloating the canonical .helix/issues.jsonl record and making distinct state transitions harder to read.
design: Keep implementation revalidation evidence, but coalesce or suppress repeated notes when the blocker reason and relevant tracker state have not changed materially. acceptance: Line 622 of scripts/helix has a fallback so that ddx agent condense failure does not abort the script. Verified by: bash -c 'set -euo pipefail; echo test | false || echo fallback' prints fallback.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: scripts/helix:622
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: The script uses set -euo pipefail. At line 622, printf ... | ddx agent condense runs as a pipeline. If ddx agent condense exits non-zero (malformed input, bug, signal), pipefail propagates the failure and set -e aborts the script — the return "$_codex_rc" on line 623 never executes. The old condense_codex_output was a local shell function immune to this class of failure. Unguarded callers (frame, evolve, align, plan, experiment, recover) would crash if using the codex agent.
Suggested fix: Guard the pipe with a fallback: printf -- '%s\n' "$_codex_raw" | ddx agent condense || printf -- '%s\n' "$_codex_raw"
title: correctness: ddx agent condense failure crashes run loop via pipefail
